sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Small bowel adenocarcinoma (SBA) often presents with a complication of the tumor rather than early, localized symptoms. The most common presenting symptom is abdominal pain, frequently described as cramping and associated with a partial or complete small bowel obstruction. For duodenal tumors, this may manifest as gastric outlet obstruction with nausea, vomiting, and early satiety. A significant proportion of patients (approximately 25% to 33%) present with occult gastrointestinal bleeding, which can lead to iron-deficiency anemia and fatigue. Other presenting complaints include unexplained weight loss, a palpable abdominal mass, or jaundice specifically for tumors of the second portion of the duodenum that obstruct the ampulla or distal bile duct. The diagnosis is often delayed because symptoms are nonspecific and can mimic more common benign gastrointestinal conditions, leading to a higher stage at diagnosis compared to colorectal cancer [SBA-A 1 of 2, Discussion MS-5].

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain
symptoms.detail: Often cramping in nature, may be intermittent if partial obstruction is present.

symptoms.name: Nausea and vomiting
symptoms.detail: May indicate gastric outlet obstruction (duodenal tumors) or high-grade small bowel obstruction.

symptoms.name: Occult gastrointestinal bleeding / Anemia
symptoms.detail: Bleeding can be chronic and occult, leading to iron-deficiency anemia, fatigue, and pallor.
symptoms.frequency: Approximately one-quarter to one-third of cases [Discussion MS-5].

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss may be due to malabsorption, anorexia, or advanced disease.

symptoms.name: Jaundice
symptoms.detail: Specifically for ampullary or periampullary duodenal tumors causing biliary obstruction.
symptoms.frequency: Occurs in a subset of duodenal cancers.

symptoms.name: Palpable abdominal mass
symptoms.detail: May be felt on physical examination, particularly in advanced or bulky disease.
symptoms.frequency: Less common at initial presentation than obstructive symptoms.

symptoms.name: Signs of anemia
symptoms.detail: Pallor, tachycardia, and fatigue from chronic blood loss.
symptoms.frequency: Common, often associated with occult GI bleeding.

symptoms.name: Signs of obstruction
symptoms.detail: Abdominal distension, tympany, and hyperactive or hypoactive bowel sounds.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Persistent or worsening pain suggests obstruction or locally advanced disease.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Vomiting of bilious material or feculent vomiting suggests significant obstruction.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Significant, unexplained weight loss (>5% body weight over 6-12 months).

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Painless jaundice suggests malignant biliary obstruction.
